speaker: interviewer
text: --lak. Sala, you were telling us how you and your family were sent to Siberia. What time of year of-- what time of year and what year was it when you arrived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived to Siberia in '40.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it in the fall?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was fall.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of the town that you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was in Novosibirsk or Oblast and [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was in charge of this town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was running it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see any authority figures? Were they Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see any of them except a couple like soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who assigned your living condition-- you know, where you were to live and you food?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were soldiers, yeah. They assigned our living. And they told-- and they showed us the kitchen, where we're going to go once a day to get our meal. And there was a little store we used to go and got our bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was a room where you lived like in a barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barrack. It was barracks [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any other housing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were surrounded by trees, big trees.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it very isolated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't. I don't think it was a labor camp. I know they took a lot of the people than I was-- I was only, like, 15 years old. So they didn't take me to hard work. We used to do, like, a-- you know, when they take off the core from the trees? When the trees were to cut off, all the people, the men, all the people, used to cut trees.

speaker: interviewee
text: Saw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cut them off. Cut off the-- the trees.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the younger ones, we used to go and take the skin off. Because they used to ship the lumber, you know, to other parts on rivers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were removing the bark?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bark. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the tree.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was removing the bark. And in summertime, we used to go to fields. And no, no, I don't-- this is we used to take off the-- the-- [INAUDIBLE] how you called it. Used to cut off from the fields the-- like, not--

speaker: interviewer
text: The wheat?

speaker: interviewee
text: The wheat. Yeah, the younger ones, the 14, 15-year-olds. Yes. That's what we used to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was like a working farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your parents ever receive any money for the work they did?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents didn't work. They were considered already older. They were in-- close to their 60s. So they didn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of things did they talk to you about when you came home? What were their concerns and their worries? My parents? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were worrying how it's-- if we will, when we will go back, and where-- when will we see the rest of the family. That was the only worry we had. And we are hungry. So we were dreaming of more food. And that- that's all the mentality it was. Will we have enough bread? You know, oh, if I would have enough bread. And I was missing my brothers. [INAUDIBLE]. Especially when my both parents passed away in short time, six months apart, I was-- when I saw a family, when I has seen some other people were with their siblings or had at least one or two of their family, I-- I envied them so much. You were lonely for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was all alone. I was all alone. I said, my, god. I have six brothers. If one would be with me, would be great. But unfortunately--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel a lot of responsibility for your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very scared that I knew they were not so young, that something is going to happen to them. And I be left alone, that was in my head. I was very scared. That's why I wanted so much to be with my older sister, so I have more support in case something happens to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a problem that they weren't working? Did you receive less food because of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it all Jewish people in this camp, this town?

speaker: interviewee
text: This town. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people in your room, were they Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, those were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any newspapers coming to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any word of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel isolated?

speaker: interviewee
text: No radio, no nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: No? Was there any entertainment in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm-hmm. I don't know what we did all day. Honest to God, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in this camp in Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over a year. When the German war broke out with the Russian, then-- they-- they-- they were-- they told us we can go. We can leave this place and go wherever we want to. But we didn't have money. It was hard. But somehow, we got away from this, from Siberia. And there were a lot of Jewish families that organized together. And they said, let's go where it's warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: If--

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went, we tried to-- you know, we had to go through rivers. We went on boats. And it took us a long time, a couple of weeks. Then we reached a town where there was a train that took us away. And we went to Tashkent. I guess it was maybe the Red Cross that helped us. I don't remember exactly everything. But we went from-- from Siberia, a town, we went on a train to Tashkent.

speaker: interviewer
text: If If you were to describe that year in Siberia, the cold, and the hunger, and the fear, what would you say impressed you the most about your time there if you had to describe it to me? What was the worst part of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The worst part, it was very cold. The snow was always very, very high. And it mostly was the hunger I would say and the loneliness that you miss your family. And we hoped that it won't last long. We didn't know if it's forever or if it's going to end. We didn't know how it's going to end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of the fact that the war was going to be a war against the Jews to the extent it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, we didn't know that's going-- that it's going to be so bad with the Jews. We didn't know that they building concentration camps. We didn't know we were like blocked out with thinking what's going to happen. Will we see our family? And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You weren't aware of the extent of what was happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: We weren't of nothing, not that I remember. Maybe somebody else was, but I wasn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened on the way to Tashkent?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the way to-- from Tashkent, we went with cattle trains. And there's-- my father would-- already was sick. He had dysentery. He started already in Siberia. But there was no place, no doctors, no medication, nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember him being sick in Siberia already. And going on those cattle trains to Tashkent, he passed away in the train. We were all laying, you know, on the floor in this wagon. And he just fall asleep. And-- and then we stopped. We told somebody that who was in charge. We told him. They stopped. And they took him off from the train. We don't know. My mother, I remember she had a white sheet. And she said please put him in that white sheet. Cover him up. And they just took him off. That was the end of my father. I remember the town where they stopped, the name of the town. But we couldn't go. Bolotnoe it was. And we couldn't go down or see what they're doing with him. That was it. They took him off. But there was a lot of people that were dying out in the train, little kids even. There was typhus. There was hunger. There were lice.

speaker: interviewee
text: No sanitary--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it took us maybe three weeks. And it was in fall. It was already cold in the trains. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: No sanitary conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: No sanitary, no. I remember when we came to Tashkent, the Red Cross, the Red Cross came in. They took our temperatures. And they checked us out. I had a temperature. My mother had a temperature. They took us right from the station to a hospital, my mother and me and many other people. A lot of them died out from the typhus was going.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when they took us to the hospital, my mother and me, and my mother was very sick. I was very sick, too. But I recovered quickly after two or three weeks. And my mother took a long time, because she was older. I guess maybe this was the case. And she was maybe in the hospital three months. They let me stay in the hospital, because they know where will I go? They were very nice about that. They let me stay. Who--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who owned this-- was this a Russian hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Russian hospital. I don't know who owned it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother and father, did they have a good-- good relationship your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. My father was a very respectful quiet man. We had-- I didn't see any problems with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother very despondent after he died?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was very upset, very depressed. And I love my mother so much that I was so happy that I have her still. So, and it didn't take long. My mother was in hospital three months. And then when she felt better, we-- they-- we had to leave the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: So where do we go? We went on the streets. There were a lot of people. We're not the only one. There were hundreds of people like us on the streets. And that we used to-- we went to a-- like a-- where the station where the trains go. How you called these? The depot?

speaker: interviewee
text: To a depot to have a roof over a-- to hide there for the night to have a roof over our head to the depots. But at night, they used to push us out. They ask us where are you going? Show me the tickets. We had no tickets, because we just want to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hide.

speaker: interviewee
text: --hide from the cold. And so they pushed us out, because we didn't have tickets. And it didn't take long. My mother got sick again.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were homeless.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were homeless, yeah. There was a Red Cross there. We went back for a piece of bread. We stayed in the line for a piece of bread, that's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were on the streets. And my mother was-- got shortly sick again, maybe a month later or even not that. And they took her to a hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were lots of kids like me in mine age. We used to go and beg for bread, for work. We used to wash-- wanted to wash floors. We help with the families that were living there. They had-- they were citizens.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I met a Jewish couple that-- there. And they were very nice to me. They said, why did you come and help me with the child? And, you know, they fed me. They gave me some old clothes. And my mother, meanwhile, was in the hospital. So on the one day-- I used to go to see her. But they wouldn't let me in, because it was infected. You know, they were afraid that I'd get infect-- infection from-- infected-- how you say it? An infection. She-- she was quarantined from you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't want you to get sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was quarantined. They didn't want-- so I couldn't see her. And she was on the second floor. But a couple weeks later, they put her down on the first floor. So I could see her through the window.

speaker: interviewee
text: But one-- a couple of days later, I came in. and the-- and the bed was made. I said, where is my mom? They said, she passed away. Somebody told me-- I don't know if it's true-- they said that they poisoned the people.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't want to bother with them, so they gave him poison. I don't know if it's true. So here I was all alone. And I even went to see her after-- you know, they took me in to see her when she was dead already.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she-- and later on, I was-- I went to this Jewish family that I made friends. And they said, well, you have to go to work. What are you going to do? And-- and so I went at-- at this factory. And I told them that I'm alone. I would like a job. And they asked me, you have a passport? And I said, no. So they said, well, you have to take out a passport, become a citizen, then you can have a job. So I took out a Russian passport. And I got the job. How old were you, Sala?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 16 and 1/2.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were all alone in a strange country?

speaker: interviewee
text: All alone. All alone. Not one-- and we were such big families. From my mother's side, so many cousins, uncles-- all alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was what you feared the most.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was hard. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So they gave me a place where to, you know, where the factory was. Not too far away, they build like barracks. And they-- and they give us to live there. They're called [NON-ENGLISH], like a dormitory. But that-- that was we only had a little bed, a little chair. And the floor was still ground. They build up so it's, you know, barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Quickly, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were a lot of people that were evacuated even from Russia, like people from Krakow-- no, Krakow is Poland-- from the Zamost, from Kharkov, from Bessarabia, from Romania. It was a big, big one place that we were all together. Some of them had sisters. Some of them had a mom. And I was all alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make friendships? Did you find people to sustain you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- we made friends. There was one girl from Lodz, from Poland. She was a little older than I am. We were all-- all made-- we were all close. We all worked many hours. I used to work night shift. And for me to sleep in the daytime was so hard, because one came in and one screamed and one talked. It was impossible to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we had to go at night to work, it was so hard. Because we were almost falling asleep by the machines. Because we didn't have rest during the day. What type of work were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: By big machines we were work textile factory. They were mostly making, like, I think it was maybe for military, like sacks, you know, sack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a bag to hold things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Big ones like to put flour in and potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know, there were so many parts. It was a big factory. They used to bring from the fields, the cotton, or linen, whatever it was, used to wash it. And made-- and I used to work on the second floor. They made like threads on big spools.

speaker: interviewer
text: You worked hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was hard, because we didn't have good rest during the day, couldn't sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you paid a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not paid by money. We just we-- again, we had some soup from the kitchen, bread, piece of bread for the day, so and so much bread. And if you were at good worker, you got once in a while, some little sugar, little jam. [NON-ENGLISH] it called, you know, if you were a good worker.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a bonus.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, I already knew what was going on a little bit in Europe. Because they-- they informed us in the factory. They gave us the news what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge of the administration in that factory? Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some men, Russian men, and some women. You know, like here, you have some--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I wasn't afraid. I was-- I didn't experience any anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they knew you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: People knew you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any danger of the Germans coming close while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We hear it so much was going on in Stalingrad and Leningrad, that they fighting. There's so many people are killed. But I don't think I heard about the concentration camps. That's just- we know that there is going on a war, a bad war, and people had been killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any communication with any of your family members from home, from Poland, your brothers or your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all. Not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to write to anyone and tell them about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you work in this factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: About five years probably. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Five years, till the war ended. 1945 when the war ended, we still-- we still worked. A year later, I was still there, almost a year. Till they came out with this that we can go home to Poland if we want to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you keep any of your religious feelings during the war? Did you celebrate any of the Jewish holidays during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all there, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other Jewish girls that you worked with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There were, but nobody observed anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rosh Hashanah came by, were you aware of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing. We had no holidays, no nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that feel to you? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I didn't even think about it. Only thing I was thinking, you know, will I ever meet somebody that will be alive that I can tell them the stories that I went through with my father, with my mother? You know, how it's sorry they should feel for me what I went through. That's what-- you know, and how are hungry we-- we were. That-- that was the only-- or, if we-- if sometime we will have enough to eat or if we will have enough bread. Our mind was occupied only with this.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war ended--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how did you hear of that news?

speaker: interviewee
text: We hear that. We hear that. It was a happy occasion. Everybody was dancing and music. We had Russian band, I guess. And it was a very happy occasion that the war ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were still living in this barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you start to hear news of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you how I start-- how I was. A short time before the war ended, I found a family that left with us on the same bus from Szczekocin. I found this family. They were not too far from Tashkent. They were in a smaller town. And they were much-- there was a whole family. There children were mine age. Their two daughters were mine age. They had a son also. And I went to them. And they took me, very nicely, in. And they felt so sorry for me. Because I told them I lost my parents, and I'm all alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were very, you know, they gave me some money. They were very nice to me. They left before me to Poland or to Germany. They left, because they were all the people.

speaker: interviewee
text: He probably had money. Because some people, you know, had-- made money on the black market. All the people handled things different than I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So when they left, they met-- they left to Germany. And they met my brother there. And they said to them, you know, I know where your sister is. She is in Tashkent and so and so.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day, they called me to the office. And they told me that I have a telegram-- that was already after the war-- that I have a telegram. It was my two brothers' name that they are alive and they wish I can come there so and so, you know, the address, if I can come real soon. So I know I have two brothers still alive. But I still couldn't go till the government will send me out. Because I didn't have any money. I have no connection. How did you feel inside when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was jumping on the-- I went on my bed, and I was jumping and dancing. It was a big holiday, you know, that I have two brothers. So at least I know I have somebody. And when they send me-- when they start sending out the people from Russia, I went. And they sent us to a-- to a town in Poland. The whole [NON-ENGLISH], they call-- the whole train went to one place, you know, they sent us. [INAUDIBLE] it was. But somehow my sister, the married one with the two children which were already not children. They were-- one was already 17. They had a son. And the daughter must be 20 after the war. Somehow, they, too, found out that I was in Tashkent. And they had some information. And One day-- one day-- they were in Krakow. They came to Krakow after the war. They found out that in this-- this day, people are coming from Tashkent. So I-- and, you know, from time to time, the-- the train stopped. And they stopped in Krakow also. They opened the doors.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I hear somebody is asking my name. So that was my nephew, my sister's son. He was maybe 17. And he said, maybe in this wagon is a Fromer, Sala Fromer? And I hear that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I jumped up from the train. I left everything. I had a little package of maybe clothes. I jumped out from the train. The train went further. But I already went with my nephew. And he took me to my sister, my brother-in-law. And I came there. We didn't see each other for seven years, six years. So I started crying and talking what happened. [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewer
text: You told her about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told them about my parents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so I was with them. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: It was quite a reunion.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was with them. And then we all went to Germany to meet-- and my two brothers were in Germany in Landsberg. That's close to Munich, a town. They were there in the DP lagers

speaker: interviewee
text: So were all-- my-- I wasn't alone anymore. I was with my sister, my brother-in-law and [INAUDIBLE] two nieces and nephew and niece. And we went to my brothers. In fact, we went to-- to-- Austria-- to-- to Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was like a [NON-ENGLISH]. It was like a temporary place for us. And my brother came from Germany to Austria to greet us to take us over to where he is in Germany, to Landsberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, your sister had been in Russia with her family also, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she survived there.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your two brothers, where-- where had they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in the concentration camps, Auschwitz, Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they somehow survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they survived. Two survived. And four wasn't that-- weren't that lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you-- did they have information on what had happened to the other brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe. I think one brother told us that he saw one brother died right after the war. He was so sick, he couldn't go on anymore. And they didn't know from all of them or my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: So two brothers and two sisters, your two girls survived, four of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Two were in Russia and two in the concentration camps. So we were all together in Landsberg, Germany. We were in the DP camps for our-- that's where I met my husband. And then we separated again. Because my brother, in 1948, he was married. You know, after the war, he got married. He had a baby. He went to Israel. And one brother was also married. They had a little girl. They went to Australia. And my sister came to United States and so did we.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how you met your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is his name, your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aron. I met Aron in Landsberg in the DP camps. In fact, somebody introduced us to each other. Somebody from his hometown, he said to me, you know, I know this guy. He's a good guy. He-- I know his family from home. I know his parents. They're good, very nice people. And he talked a lot about-- good about him. And I said, oh, maybe I should decide. Because I was scared for somebody that's going to be not nice to me. So I decided we were-- you know, we knew each other a short time. And I wanted to have my own place, my own family. And that's-- we got married in '48. I came '47 to Landsberg. I mean, to this DP camp. Were all together for a short time, but then we separated again. This is [INAUDIBLE] brother, except my sister. Well, the brother came here, too, a little bit later [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that hard for you when you all split up again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I begged my brother. I said, don't go to Australia. Come to United States, so we all-- especially, I love this brother so much. But his wife had family in Australia. And she wanted very-- she wanted to go to Australia. So, he went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just ask him when he was here two months ago-- he was here. I said, oh, it's so bad that you're so far away. We-- we have such a separated life. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We can't see each other. Because we really love each other. But that's how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back shortly when I was-- after the war, I went back to my hometown. And I went back to our house where we used to live. I went back to the neighbors, the Polish people. And I thought maybe there is some pictures or anything left. But nothing was left. The house was there. There was like a six apartment house where we used to live. And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: But none of your possessions were left?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, absolutely nothing.
